questions.q: Which OSI layer is responsible for logical addressing and packet routing?
questions.explanation: Layer 3 (Network) handles IP addresses and routing. Routers operate here.

questions.q: Which OSI layer converts data between application formats and handles encryption/decryption?
questions.explanation: Layer 6 (Presentation) handles data translation, formatting, and encryption. TLS operates here.

questions.q: Which OSI layer manages connection establishment, maintenance, and termination between applications?
questions.explanation: Layer 5 (Session) establishes, manages, and terminates sessions. NetBIOS and RPC operate here.

questions.q: At which OSI layer do switches primarily operate?
questions.explanation: Switches operate at Layer 2 (Data Link) and use MAC addresses to forward frames.

questions.q: What is the PDU (Protocol Data Unit) at the Transport layer?
questions.explanation: Transport layer PDU = Segment. Network=Packet, Data Link=Frame, Physical=Bit.

questions.q: ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) operates at which OSI layer?
questions.explanation: ARP resolves IP addresses to MAC addresses and operates at Layer 2 (Data Link).

questions.q: Which OSI layer do hubs operate at?
questions.explanation: Hubs operate at Layer 1 (Physical). They simply repeat signals to all ports — no intelligence.

questions.q: What is the PDU name at the Data Link layer?
questions.explanation: Data Link layer PDU = Frame. Frames include MAC addresses for local delivery.

questions.q: Which OSI layer is responsible for end-to-end error correction, flow control, and sequencing?
questions.explanation: Layer 4 (Transport) handles reliable delivery — TCP provides error correction, flow control, and sequencing.

questions.q: HTTPS operates at which OSI layer?
questions.explanation: HTTPS is an application protocol (Layer 7). Note: TLS encryption used by HTTPS is Layer 6.

questions.q: A packet filter firewall operates primarily at which OSI layer?
questions.explanation: Basic packet filter firewalls examine IP headers at Layer 3 (Network layer).

questions.q: A stateful inspection firewall tracks connection state at which OSI layer?
questions.explanation: Stateful firewalls track TCP/UDP sessions at Layer 4 (Transport layer).

questions.q: What is the PDU at the Network layer?
questions.explanation: Network layer PDU = Packet. IP packets contain source and destination IP addresses.

questions.q: Which OSI layer does ICMP (used by ping) operate at?
questions.explanation: ICMP operates at Layer 3 (Network). Ping uses ICMP Echo Request and Echo Reply messages.

questions.q: Which mnemonic correctly describes OSI layers from Layer 7 to Layer 1?
options: All People Seem To Need Data Processing
All People Should Try New Data Paths
All Pros Study TCP Networks Daily Periodically
Avoid Paying Salaries To Network Data Providers
questions.answer: All People Seem To Need Data Processing
questions.explanation: A-P-S-T-N-D-P = Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical (top to bottom).

questions.q: Which layer handles physical addressing using MAC addresses?
questions.explanation: MAC addresses are used at Layer 2 (Data Link) for local delivery within the same network segment.

questions.q: A Next-Generation Firewall (NGFW) inspects application content. Which OSI layer does it operate at?
questions.explanation: NGFWs perform deep packet inspection up through Layer 7 (Application layer), identifying specific applications and content.

questions.q: Which OSI layer uses IP addresses?
questions.explanation: IP addresses are logical addresses used at Layer 3 (Network layer). MAC addresses are used at Layer 2.

questions.q: Which OSI layer is responsible for the physical transmission of raw bits over a medium?
questions.explanation: Layer 1 (Physical) deals with raw bit transmission over cables, fiber, or wireless signals.

questions.q: At which OSI layer does the SSL/TLS protocol primarily operate?
questions.explanation: TLS/SSL handles encryption and data format conversion — these are Presentation layer (Layer 6) functions.

questions.q: How many layers does the TCP/IP model have?
questions.explanation: TCP/IP has 4 layers: Application, Transport, Network (Internet), and Network Interface (Link).

questions.q: Which TCP/IP layer corresponds to OSI layers 5, 6, and 7?
questions.explanation: The TCP/IP Application layer combines OSI Session, Presentation, and Application layers.

questions.q: What is the main difference between TCP and UDP?
options: TCP is faster, UDP is reliable
TCP is reliable and connection-oriented; UDP is unreliable and connectionless
TCP uses IP; UDP does not
TCP is for web traffic only
questions.answer: TCP is reliable and connection-oriented; UDP is unreliable and connectionless
questions.explanation: TCP guarantees delivery with acknowledgments; UDP sends data without verification — faster but no guarantees.

questions.q: Which protocol is used by streaming video and online gaming due to its low overhead?
questions.explanation: UDP has lower overhead than TCP — no handshake, no ACKs. Perfect for real-time media where speed matters more than perfect delivery.

questions.q: What is the TCP three-way handshake sequence?
questions.explanation: Client sends SYN → Server responds SYN-ACK → Client completes with ACK. This establishes a TCP connection.

questions.q: Which TCP/IP layer is responsible for routing packets between networks?
questions.explanation: The TCP/IP Internet layer (equivalent to OSI Layer 3) handles IP routing between networks.

questions.q: Which protocol at the Network layer reports errors and sends diagnostic messages (used by ping)?
questions.explanation: ICMP (Internet Control Message Protocol) sends error and diagnostic messages. Ping uses ICMP Echo Request/Reply.

questions.q: ARP is found in which TCP/IP layer?
questions.explanation: While ARP resolves Layer 2 MAC addresses from Layer 3 IPs, it's classified in the Internet (Network) layer of TCP/IP.

questions.q: Which characteristic describes UDP?
questions.explanation: UDP provides best-effort delivery — fast, but doesn't guarantee or verify delivery.

questions.q: What does NAT (Network Address Translation) do?
options: Translates private IP addresses to public IP addresses
questions.answer: Translates private IP addresses to public IP addresses
questions.explanation: NAT allows multiple devices with private IPs to share one public IP. Typically performed by a router.

questions.q: Which device operates at Layer 3 and routes packets between different networks?
questions.explanation: Routers operate at Layer 3, use IP addresses, and route packets between different networks.

questions.q: Which device broadcasts data to ALL ports regardless of destination?
questions.explanation: Hubs are Layer 1 devices that repeat signals to all connected ports without intelligence — creating collision domains.

questions.q: Which device uses MAC addresses to forward data ONLY to the intended destination port?
questions.explanation: Switches maintain a MAC address table and forward frames only to the correct port — reducing collisions.

questions.q: Which device converts digital LAN signals to analog for telephone line transmission?
questions.explanation: A modem (modulator-demodulator) converts between digital and analog signals for telephone line transmission.

questions.q: Which device connects two different network segments and makes simple forwarding decisions without full routing?
questions.explanation: Bridges connect two network segments at Layer 2 and make basic forwarding decisions — simpler than routers.

questions.q: Which device is responsible for implementing NAT and connecting a home network to the internet?
questions.explanation: Routers implement NAT, translating private IPs to the public IP for internet access.

questions.q: A wireless access point (WAP) connects wireless devices to which type of network?
questions.explanation: A WAP bridges wireless devices to the wired LAN infrastructure.

questions.q: Which device amplifies and retransmits signals to extend network range without changing data?
questions.explanation: Repeaters operate at Layer 1 — they regenerate signals to extend cable length without analyzing data.

questions.q: A range extender for Wi-Fi is a type of which device?
questions.explanation: Range extenders are wireless repeaters — they take a Wi-Fi signal and re-broadcast it to extend coverage.

questions.q: Which early computing device allowed users to send commands to a mainframe without local processing?
questions.explanation: Dumb terminals had no local processing — they sent keystrokes to the mainframe and displayed results.

questions.q: Which network type covers a small geographic area like a home or office?
questions.explanation: LAN (Local Area Network) covers a small area — home, office, or building.

questions.q: Which network type connects devices within personal range (under 10 meters) like Bluetooth?
questions.explanation: PAN (Personal Area Network) covers a very short range — Bluetooth headphones, smartwatches, etc.

questions.q: Which network spans a city or metropolitan area?
questions.explanation: MAN (Metropolitan Area Network) covers a city — larger than LAN, smaller than WAN.

questions.q: The internet is an example of which network type?
questions.explanation: WAN (Wide Area Network) spans large geographic areas. The internet is the largest WAN.

questions.q: Which type of network uses a switch to create a logical segment within a physical LAN?
questions.explanation: VLANs (Virtual LANs) use switches to segment a network logically without physical separation.

questions.q: A WLAN uses which technology for connectivity?
questions.explanation: WLAN (Wireless LAN) uses radio signals (Wi-Fi) instead of physical cables.

questions.q: A company uses a switch to separate HR and Engineering departments on the same physical network. This is an example of?
questions.explanation: VLANs provide logical separation on the same physical switch infrastructure — common for department isolation.

questions.q: VLAN trunk ports use which standard for tagging frames with VLAN IDs?
questions.explanation: IEEE 802.1Q adds a 4-byte VLAN tag to Ethernet frames on trunk ports carrying multiple VLANs.

questions.q: Which network type would connect multiple branch offices of a corporation across cities?
questions.explanation: WAN connects geographically dispersed locations — branch offices across cities or countries.

questions.q: Which network topology connects every device to every other device?
questions.explanation: Mesh topology provides full redundancy — every device connects to every other. Best fault tolerance.

questions.q: Which topology is recommended to survive multiple connection failures?
questions.explanation: Mesh provides redundant paths — if one link fails, traffic reroutes. Used in critical infrastructure.

questions.q: Which topology uses a central switch or hub that all devices connect to?
questions.explanation: Star topology: all devices connect to a central device. Easy to manage but single point of failure at center.

questions.q: In a ring topology, what happens if one device or link fails?
questions.explanation: Traditional ring topology is a single point of failure — one break disrupts the entire ring.

questions.q: Which topology uses a single shared cable that all devices connect to?
questions.explanation: Bus topology: all devices share a single backbone cable. Simple but prone to collisions and single point of failure.

questions.q: Which topology is most common in modern Ethernet networks?
questions.explanation: Modern Ethernet networks use star topology with a switch at the center.

questions.q: Which port does HTTP use?
questions.explanation: HTTP uses port 80. HTTPS uses port 443. Remember: 80 = unencrypted web.

questions.q: Which port does HTTPS use?
questions.explanation: HTTPS uses port 443 for encrypted web traffic (HTTP over TLS/SSL).

questions.q: SSH uses which port?
questions.explanation: SSH (Secure Shell) uses port 22 for encrypted remote access. Replace Telnet port 23.

questions.q: Which port does FTP use for data transfer?
questions.explanation: FTP uses port 21 for control/commands and port 20 for actual data transfer.

questions.q: DNS resolves domain names to IP addresses using which port?
questions.explanation: DNS uses port 53 (both TCP and UDP). TCP for zone transfers, UDP for standard queries.

questions.q: Which port does SMTP (email sending) use?
questions.explanation: SMTP uses port 25 for sending email. Port 110 = POP3, Port 143 = IMAP.

questions.q: DHCP server listens on which port?
questions.explanation: DHCP server uses port 67. DHCP client uses port 68. DHCP dynamically assigns IP addresses.

questions.q: TFTP uses which transport protocol and port?
questions.explanation: TFTP uses UDP port 69 — lightweight, no authentication, used for firmware updates and network booting.

questions.q: Telnet uses which port and what is its main security weakness?
questions.explanation: Telnet uses port 23 and sends all data unencrypted — replaced by SSH for secure remote access.

questions.q: SFTP provides secure file transfer by running over which protocol?
questions.explanation: SFTP (SSH File Transfer Protocol) runs over SSH on port 22. Not related to FTP despite the name.

questions.q: Which port does SNMP use for network device management?
questions.explanation: SNMP uses UDP port 161 for queries and port 162 for traps (alerts sent from device to manager).

questions.q: RDP (Remote Desktop Protocol) uses which port?
questions.explanation: RDP uses TCP port 3389. It provides graphical remote desktop access to Windows systems.

questions.q: Which protocol is used to receive email (downloads messages from server)?
questions.explanation: POP3 (Post Office Protocol 3) on port 110 downloads email. IMAP (port 143) keeps email on server.

questions.q: NTP (Network Time Protocol) synchronizes clocks using which port?
questions.explanation: NTP uses UDP port 123 to synchronize device clocks — critical for security logs and Kerberos authentication.

questions.q: Which protocol dynamically assigns IP addresses to devices on a network?
questions.explanation: DHCP (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol) automatically assigns IP, subnet mask, gateway, and DNS.

questions.q: Which command tests basic connectivity between your computer and another host?
questions.explanation: Ping sends ICMP Echo Requests and measures if a host responds. Basic connectivity test.

questions.q: Which command shows your computer's IP address, subnet mask, and default gateway on Windows?
questions.explanation: ipconfig displays network interface configuration on Windows. Use 'ipconfig /all' for full details including DNS.

questions.q: Which command shows the path packets take to reach a destination and where delays occur?
questions.explanation: tracert (Windows) / traceroute (Linux) shows each hop packets take and the latency at each router.

questions.q: Which command displays active network connections, listening ports, and network statistics?
questions.explanation: netstat shows active TCP connections, listening ports, and network statistics. Useful for spotting suspicious connections.

questions.q: Which command queries DNS servers to look up IP addresses for domain names?
questions.explanation: nslookup queries DNS to resolve domain names to IPs (or reverse lookup). Used to troubleshoot DNS issues.

questions.q: You need to see if google.com is reachable. Which command would you use?
questions.explanation: Both ping and tracert would work, but tracert also shows each hop. However, ping is the simplest test for reachability.

questions.q: A user has no internet but can ping their default gateway. Which command would help identify if DNS is the problem?
questions.explanation: If you can ping the gateway but not websites, DNS resolution is likely failing. nslookup tests DNS lookup.

questions.q: Which command shows current ARP table entries (IP to MAC mappings) on Windows?
questions.explanation: 'arp -a' displays the ARP cache — learned IP-to-MAC address mappings from Layer 2 resolution.

questions.q: On Linux/Mac, which command is equivalent to Windows 'ipconfig'?
questions.explanation: ifconfig displays network interface configuration on Linux/macOS. 'ip addr' is the modern replacement.

questions.q: Which command shows the local routing table on Windows?
questions.explanation: 'netstat -r' displays the IP routing table. Also accessible via 'route print' on Windows.

questions.q: Which Wi-Fi security protocol is considered BROKEN and should never be used?
questions.explanation: WEP (Wired Equivalent Privacy) uses RC4 and is easily cracked in minutes. Never use WEP.

questions.q: WPA2 uses which encryption algorithm?
questions.explanation: WPA2 uses AES (Advanced Encryption Standard) — much stronger than WPA's TKIP or WEP's RC4.

questions.q: Which wireless security standard is the strongest and most current?
questions.explanation: WPA3 is the newest and strongest. It uses 192-bit encryption for enterprise and SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals) for personal.

questions.q: An attacker sends deauthentication frames to kick users off a Wi-Fi network. This is a?
questions.explanation: Deauth attacks exploit 802.11 management frame vulnerabilities to forcibly disconnect wireless clients.

questions.q: Connecting Bluetooth devices without authorization to send unwanted messages is called?
questions.explanation: Bluejacking sends unsolicited messages to Bluetooth-enabled devices without pairing. Bluesnarfing is data theft.

questions.q: An attacker sets up a fake access point that mimics a legitimate one. This is called?
questions.explanation: An evil twin is a malicious AP with the same SSID as a legitimate network, designed to intercept traffic.

questions.q: WPA used which encryption algorithm (before WPA2)?
questions.explanation: WPA used TKIP (Temporal Key Integrity Protocol) with RC4 — an improvement over WEP but still weaker than WPA2's AES.

questions.q: Which 802.11 standard provides port-based network access control and authentication?
questions.explanation: 802.1X provides port-based authentication — used with RADIUS servers to authenticate users before allowing network access.

questions.q: An attacker drives around logging open Wi-Fi networks is doing what?
questions.explanation: War driving involves searching for Wi-Fi networks from a moving vehicle, typically to find unsecured networks.

questions.q: Which CIA principle ensures data is only accessible to authorized users?
questions.explanation: Confidentiality protects data from unauthorized access. Enforced via encryption, access controls, and authentication.

questions.q: Which CIA principle ensures data has not been tampered with or altered?
questions.explanation: Integrity ensures data accuracy — data hasn't been modified in transit or at rest. Enforced via hashing (MD5, SHA).

questions.q: A DDoS attack that takes a website offline primarily violates which CIA principle?
questions.explanation: Availability means systems and data are accessible when needed. DDoS attacks overwhelm systems, making them unavailable.

questions.q: Encrypting sensitive files stored on a server best addresses which CIA principle?
questions.explanation: Encryption prevents unauthorized access to data — directly supporting Confidentiality.

questions.q: A hash function like SHA-256 verifies that a downloaded file wasn't corrupted. Which principle is this?
questions.explanation: Hash verification confirms data hasn't changed — ensuring Integrity. If hashes match, data is unaltered.

questions.q: Implementing RAID storage and regular backups primarily supports which CIA principle?
questions.explanation: RAID and backups ensure systems remain available even if hardware fails — supporting Availability.

questions.q: A user changes a salary record in the HR database without authorization. Which principle is violated?
questions.explanation: Unauthorized modification violates Integrity — the accuracy and trustworthiness of data.

questions.q: Non-repudiation means?
options: A user cannot deny performing an action
Access is restricted to authorized users
questions.answer: A user cannot deny performing an action
questions.explanation: Non-repudiation ensures users can't deny their actions — typically enforced with digital signatures and audit logs.

questions.q: Which CIA principle is most directly supported by multi-factor authentication (MFA)?
questions.explanation: MFA verifies identity before granting access — protecting data confidentiality from unauthorized access.

questions.q: Data classification determines which CIA triad element?
options: What level of confidentiality data requires
questions.answer: What level of confidentiality data requires
questions.explanation: Data classification (public, internal, confidential, secret) determines the confidentiality controls required.

questions.q: What does the first 'A' in AAA stand for?
questions.explanation: AAA = Authentication (who are you?), Authorization (what can you do?), Accounting (what did you do?).

questions.q: Which AAA component determines what resources a user can access after login?
questions.explanation: Authorization defines what authenticated users are permitted to access or do — enforced by ACLs, permissions, RBAC.

questions.q: Which AAA component tracks and logs user activities for audit purposes?
questions.explanation: Accounting (also called Auditing) records what users did — login times, commands run, data accessed.

questions.q: RADIUS is a protocol that provides which functionality?
questions.explanation: RADIUS (Remote Authentication Dial-In User Service) provides centralized AAA for network access — used with 802.1X and VPN.

questions.q: Entering a username and password is an example of which AAA component?
questions.explanation: Authentication verifies identity — username/password, biometrics, smart cards, or tokens.

questions.q: An RBAC policy that prevents a clerk from accessing payroll data is an example of?
questions.explanation: Authorization controls what an authenticated user can do — role-based access controls restrict the clerk to their role's permissions.

questions.q: Security logs showing a user accessed specific files at specific times represent which AAA component?
questions.explanation: Accounting/auditing creates logs of user activity — essential for incident investigation and compliance.

questions.q: Multi-factor authentication (MFA) combines which types of factors?
options: Something you know + something you have/are
questions.answer: Something you know + something you have/are
questions.explanation: MFA combines: something you know (password), something you have (token/phone), something you are (biometric).

questions.q: Which encryption type uses the SAME key for encryption and decryption?
questions.explanation: Symmetric encryption uses one shared key for both encryption and decryption. Fast, but key distribution is a challenge.

questions.q: Which encryption type uses a PUBLIC key to encrypt and a PRIVATE key to decrypt?
questions.explanation: Asymmetric encryption uses a key pair. Anyone can encrypt with the public key; only the private key holder can decrypt.

questions.q: Which is a common symmetric encryption algorithm?
questions.explanation: AES (Advanced Encryption Standard) is the most widely used symmetric algorithm. RSA and ECC are asymmetric.

questions.q: RSA is which type of encryption?
questions.explanation: RSA is an asymmetric algorithm using public/private key pairs. Commonly used for secure key exchange and digital signatures.

questions.q: A hash function like SHA-256 is unique because it is?
options: Produces the same output for different inputs
A one-way function — output cannot be reversed
questions.answer: A one-way function — output cannot be reversed
questions.explanation: Hash functions are one-way — you can't reverse a hash to get the original input. Used to verify integrity.

questions.q: Which hashing algorithm is considered BROKEN and should no longer be used for security?
questions.explanation: MD5 has known collision vulnerabilities — multiple inputs can produce the same hash. Use SHA-256 or better.

questions.q: In asymmetric encryption, what does Alice use to send Bob an encrypted message only Bob can read?
questions.explanation: To send a secret message to Bob, Alice encrypts with Bob's PUBLIC key. Only Bob's private key can decrypt it.

questions.q: What is a digital signature used for?
options: Verifying identity and ensuring message integrity
questions.answer: Verifying identity and ensuring message integrity
questions.explanation: Digital signatures verify the sender's identity (authentication) and confirm data wasn't tampered with (integrity).

questions.q: Which protocol provides encrypted communication for web browsers?
questions.explanation: TLS (Transport Layer Security) encrypts web traffic. HTTPS = HTTP over TLS.

questions.q: IPsec operates at which OSI layer?
questions.explanation: IPsec secures IP communications at Layer 3 (Network layer). Used for VPN tunnels.

questions.q: WPA2 uses which specific AES mode for wireless encryption?
questions.explanation: WPA2 uses AES in CCMP (Counter Mode with Cipher Block Chaining Message Authentication Code Protocol) mode.

questions.q: Which is an advantage of asymmetric over symmetric encryption?
options: No need to share a secret key
questions.answer: No need to share a secret key
questions.explanation: With asymmetric encryption, you publish your public key — no need to securely share a secret. Solves the key distribution problem.

questions.q: In Discretionary Access Control (DAC), who determines access permissions?
questions.explanation: DAC — the resource owner decides who gets access. Like file permissions in Windows/Linux where the owner sets them.

questions.q: Which access control model enforces permissions based on security clearance levels and data classifications (government-style)?
questions.explanation: MAC (Mandatory Access Control) — the OS/admin enforces labels (Top Secret, Secret, Confidential). Users can't change permissions.

questions.q: Which access control model grants permissions based on a user's job function or role?
questions.explanation: RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) — users get permissions based on their role (admin, manager, clerk). Very common in enterprise.

questions.q: Least privilege is a security principle that means?
options: Users have no access by default
Users get the minimum permissions needed to do their job
Admins have less access than users
Access is always revoked after 30 days
questions.answer: Users get the minimum permissions needed to do their job
questions.explanation: Least privilege limits damage from compromised accounts by ensuring users only have the access they truly need.

questions.q: An Access Control List (ACL) is used by which device to permit or deny network traffic?
questions.explanation: ACLs on routers and firewalls define rules for which traffic to allow or deny based on IPs, ports, or protocols.

questions.q: Which access control model is used in Windows file system where file owners set permissions?
questions.explanation: Windows NTFS file permissions are DAC — the file owner (or admin) discretionarily sets who can read/write/execute.

questions.q: Separation of duties is a security control that prevents?
options: A single person from having too much control over critical processes
questions.answer: A single person from having too much control over critical processes
questions.explanation: Separation of duties splits critical tasks between multiple people — prevents fraud (e.g., same person can't both create and approve payments).

questions.q: A smurf attack works by?
options: Sending ICMP broadcasts with a spoofed source IP to flood the victim
Exploiting buffer overflow in web apps
Injecting SQL into a database query
questions.answer: Sending ICMP broadcasts with a spoofed source IP to flood the victim
questions.explanation: Smurf attack: attacker sends ICMP Echo Requests to a broadcast address with spoofed source IP. All hosts reply to victim, overwhelming it.

questions.q: A teardrop attack works by?
options: Sending overlapping, malformed IP fragments that crash the victim
questions.answer: Sending overlapping, malformed IP fragments that crash the victim
questions.explanation: Teardrop sends IP fragments with overlapping offset values — the target's TCP/IP stack crashes when trying to reassemble them.

questions.q: A ping of death attack sends?
options: ICMP Echo Requests that exceed the maximum packet size of 65,535 bytes
Pings using TCP instead of ICMP
questions.answer: ICMP Echo Requests that exceed the maximum packet size of 65,535 bytes
questions.explanation: Ping of death sends oversized ICMP packets — buffer overflow crashes or freezes the target.

questions.q: A Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) attack intercepts communication by?
options: Positioning between two parties without their knowledge
questions.answer: Positioning between two parties without their knowledge
questions.explanation: MitM places the attacker between victim and destination, intercepting or modifying all communication.

questions.q: ARP spoofing/poisoning is used to perform which type of attack?
questions.explanation: ARP spoofing sends fake ARP replies to poison ARP caches — redirecting traffic through the attacker's machine (MitM).

questions.q: SQL injection attacks target which type of vulnerability?
questions.explanation: SQL injection inserts malicious SQL code into input fields — manipulates or extracts database content.

questions.q: A SYN flood attack exploits which protocol's three-way handshake?
questions.explanation: SYN flood sends many SYN packets but never completes the handshake — exhausts server connection table (DoS).

questions.q: DNS poisoning (DNS cache poisoning) redirects users by?
options: Injecting false DNS records to map a domain to a malicious IP
questions.answer: Injecting false DNS records to map a domain to a malicious IP
questions.explanation: DNS poisoning corrupts the DNS cache — users get directed to malicious sites even when typing legitimate URLs.

questions.q: VLAN hopping allows an attacker to?
options: Access traffic on a VLAN they shouldn't have access to
Create new VLANs on a switch
questions.answer: Access traffic on a VLAN they shouldn't have access to
questions.explanation: VLAN hopping exploits trunk port configurations (DTP or double-tagging) to send frames to unauthorized VLANs.

questions.q: A replay attack works by?
options: Sending duplicate network traffic to impersonate a legitimate user
questions.answer: Sending duplicate network traffic to impersonate a legitimate user
questions.explanation: Replay attacks capture valid data transmissions and re-send them to trick the receiver into accepting them as legitimate.

questions.q: A DoS (Denial of Service) attack differs from DDoS (Distributed DoS) because?
options: DoS comes from ONE source; DDoS uses many compromised systems
DoS targets web apps; DDoS targets networks
questions.answer: DoS comes from ONE source; DDoS uses many compromised systems
questions.explanation: DDoS uses botnets (hundreds/thousands of compromised machines) to overwhelm a target — much harder to block than single-source DoS.

questions.q: Bluesnarfing is different from Bluejacking because?
options: Bluesnarfing sends messages; Bluejacking steals data
Bluesnarfing steals data from Bluetooth devices; Bluejacking just sends unsolicited messages
Bluesnarfing is Wi-Fi; Bluejacking is Bluetooth
questions.answer: Bluesnarfing steals data from Bluetooth devices; Bluejacking just sends unsolicited messages
questions.explanation: Bluejacking is annoying but harmless. Bluesnarfing actually accesses and steals data from Bluetooth devices.

questions.q: A phishing attack primarily exploits?
questions.explanation: Phishing uses deceptive emails or websites to trick users into revealing credentials. It's a social engineering attack.

questions.q: Cross-site scripting (XSS) attacks inject malicious scripts into?
options: Web pages viewed by other users
questions.answer: Web pages viewed by other users
questions.explanation: XSS injects client-side scripts into web pages — when other users view the page, the script executes in their browser.

questions.q: An on-path attack (formerly MitM) that poisons ARP caches works at which OSI layer?
questions.explanation: ARP operates at Layer 2. ARP poisoning corrupts MAC address tables — a Layer 2 attack vector.

questions.q: A zero-day attack exploits?
options: Unknown vulnerabilities with no available patch
questions.answer: Unknown vulnerabilities with no available patch
questions.explanation: Zero-day = unknown vulnerability. 'Zero days' since the vendor has had zero days to patch it — extremely dangerous.

questions.q: Ransomware primarily attacks which CIA principle?
questions.explanation: Ransomware encrypts files (violating Availability by making them inaccessible) and may exfiltrate data (Confidentiality).

questions.q: A password spraying attack differs from brute force because?
options: It uses common passwords against many accounts instead of many passwords against one
It only works on web apps
questions.answer: It uses common passwords against many accounts instead of many passwords against one
questions.explanation: Password spraying tries a few common passwords across many accounts — avoids lockout policies that trigger on repeated attempts to one account.

questions.q: What is the primary difference between IDS and IPS?
options: IDS is hardware; IPS is software
IDS only detects and alerts; IPS detects AND actively blocks threats
IDS is for networks; IPS is for hosts
questions.answer: IDS only detects and alerts; IPS detects AND actively blocks threats
questions.explanation: IDS = Intrusion Detection System (passive — logs and alerts). IPS = Intrusion Prevention System (active — blocks threats inline).

questions.q: A stateful firewall tracks what information?
options: The state of active network connections
questions.answer: The state of active network connections
questions.explanation: Stateful firewalls maintain a connection table — tracking TCP state (SYN, ESTABLISHED, CLOSE) to make smarter allow/deny decisions.

questions.q: A packet filtering firewall makes decisions based on?
options: Source/destination IP and port in packet headers
questions.answer: Source/destination IP and port in packet headers
questions.explanation: Basic packet filters examine Layer 3/4 headers — IP addresses and port numbers. No state or content inspection.

questions.q: An NGFW (Next-Generation Firewall) differs from a traditional firewall because it?
options: Provides application-level inspection and user identity awareness
questions.answer: Provides application-level inspection and user identity awareness
questions.explanation: NGFWs perform deep packet inspection at Layer 7 — identifying specific applications, users, and content beyond just ports/IPs.

questions.q: A HIDS (Host-based IDS) differs from NIDS (Network IDS) because?
options: HIDS monitors a single host's activity; NIDS monitors network traffic
HIDS is passive; NIDS is active
questions.answer: HIDS monitors a single host's activity; NIDS monitors network traffic
questions.explanation: HIDS runs on individual computers (monitors logs, files, processes). NIDS monitors network traffic from a central point.

questions.q: Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) on a switch defends against which attack?
questions.explanation: DAI validates ARP packets against a DHCP snooping binding table — blocks forged ARP replies used in ARP poisoning attacks.

questions.q: A DMZ (Demilitarized Zone) in network architecture places what between two firewalls?
questions.explanation: DMZ hosts public-facing services — protected from internal network. External users access DMZ; internal network stays isolated.

questions.q: Signature-based IDS detects threats by?
options: Comparing activity against known attack patterns/signatures
questions.answer: Comparing activity against known attack patterns/signatures
questions.explanation: Signature-based IDS matches traffic against a database of known attack signatures. Fast but can't detect novel attacks.

questions.q: Anomaly-based IDS works by?
options: Establishing a baseline of normal behavior and alerting on deviations
questions.answer: Establishing a baseline of normal behavior and alerting on deviations
questions.explanation: Anomaly-based IDS learns 'normal' and alerts when behavior deviates. Can detect new attacks but may generate false positives.

questions.q: What does a VPN primarily provide?
options: Encrypted tunnel for secure remote access over public networks
questions.answer: Encrypted tunnel for secure remote access over public networks
questions.explanation: VPN creates an encrypted tunnel over the internet — allows remote workers to securely access corporate resources.

questions.q: IPsec operates in which two modes?
questions.explanation: IPsec Transport mode encrypts only the payload. Tunnel mode encrypts the entire IP packet — used for VPNs between networks.

questions.q: SSL/TLS VPN is typically accessed through?
questions.explanation: SSL VPNs use standard HTTPS (port 443) — accessible via browser without special client software. Very flexible for remote access.

questions.q: Which protocol is commonly used for site-to-site VPN connections between offices?
questions.explanation: IPsec is commonly used for site-to-site VPNs — creating encrypted tunnels between two network gateways.

questions.q: Which cable type uses light pulses and is immune to electromagnetic interference?
questions.explanation: Fiber optic transmits data as light — immune to EMI, supports very long distances and high speeds. Used in backbone networks.

questions.q: Cat5e cable supports what maximum speed?
questions.explanation: Cat5e supports up to 1 Gbps (1000BASE-T) over 100 meters. Cat6 supports 10 Gbps up to 55 meters.

questions.q: Cat6 cable provides 10 Gbps over what maximum distance?
questions.explanation: Cat6 supports 10 Gbps (10GBASE-T) up to 55 meters. At 100 meters, Cat6 drops to 1 Gbps.

questions.q: Which cable type is used for cable TV and internet distribution?
questions.explanation: Coaxial cable (coax) has a copper core with shielding — used for cable TV, internet (DOCSIS), and older Ethernet.

questions.q: UTP stands for what type of network cable?
questions.explanation: UTP (Unshielded Twisted Pair) is the most common Ethernet cable. Cat5e, Cat6, Cat6a are all UTP types.

questions.q: T1 lines provide which bandwidth?
questions.explanation: T1 = 1.544 Mbps — dedicated leased line. T3 = 44.736 Mbps. Used for WAN connections before fiber became common.

questions.q: Which cloud deployment model provides services to a single organization only?
questions.explanation: Private cloud is dedicated to one organization — provides more control and security but higher cost.

questions.q: Which cloud model allows cloud resources to be shared among several organizations with similar needs?
questions.explanation: Community cloud is shared between organizations with common concerns (e.g., government agencies, healthcare).

questions.q: Which cloud service model provides virtual machines and storage (infrastructure) to build your own solutions?
questions.explanation: IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service) provides raw compute, storage, networking. You manage OS and apps. Example: AWS EC2.

questions.q: Office 365 / Google Workspace are examples of which cloud service model?
questions.explanation: SaaS (Software as a Service) delivers ready-to-use applications. Provider manages everything.

questions.q: Which cloud deployment combines private and public cloud?
questions.explanation: Hybrid cloud combines private (sensitive data) and public cloud (scalable workloads) with integration between them.

questions.q: How many bits are in an IPv4 address?
questions.explanation: IPv4 addresses are 32 bits long, typically written in 4 octets (e.g., 192.168.1.1).

questions.q: What does the subnet mask /24 mean in CIDR notation?
questions.explanation: /24 means the first 24 bits are the network portion. The remaining 8 bits are for hosts (2^8 = 256, minus 2 = 254 usable hosts).

questions.q: The IP address 192.168.1.0/24 — how many usable host addresses are available?
questions.explanation: A /24 has 2^8 = 256 addresses. Subtract 2 (network address and broadcast address) = 254 usable hosts.

questions.q: What class of IP address is 10.0.0.0?
questions.explanation: Class A: 1-126.x.x.x. 10.x.x.x is Class A private space. Class B: 128-191.x.x.x. Class C: 192-223.x.x.x.

questions.q: Which IP range is reserved for private use at Class B?
questions.explanation: Private IP ranges: Class A = 10.x.x.x, Class B = 172.16–172.31.x.x, Class C = 192.168.x.x.

questions.q: The subnet mask 255.255.255.0 is equivalent to which CIDR notation?
questions.explanation: 255.255.255.0 in binary = 24 ones followed by 8 zeros = /24 CIDR notation.

questions.q: What is the purpose of subnetting?
options: To divide a network into smaller manageable segments for security and efficiency
questions.answer: To divide a network into smaller manageable segments for security and efficiency
questions.explanation: Subnetting divides networks into segments — improves security, reduces broadcast domains, and organizes the network.

questions.q: The IP address 169.254.x.x is called?
questions.explanation: 169.254.x.x is assigned automatically when DHCP fails — APIPA. Indicates a DHCP connectivity problem.

questions.q: The loopback address 127.0.0.1 is used to?
options: Test the local TCP/IP stack on a device
questions.answer: Test the local TCP/IP stack on a device
questions.explanation: 127.0.0.1 (localhost) loops back to the device itself — used to test if the local TCP/IP stack is working.

questions.q: Which malware type spreads by attaching itself to legitimate programs?
questions.explanation: A virus attaches to or inserts itself into legitimate files. It needs a host program and user action to spread.

questions.q: Which malware type self-replicates across networks WITHOUT needing a host file?
questions.explanation: Worms self-replicate and spread autonomously across networks — no user action or host file needed. WannaCry was a worm.

questions.q: A Trojan horse malware is defined as?
options: Malware that pretends to be legitimate software
questions.answer: Malware that pretends to be legitimate software
questions.explanation: Trojans disguise themselves as useful software — once installed, perform malicious actions. Named after the Trojan Horse story.

questions.q: Ransomware encrypts a victim's files and?
questions.explanation: Ransomware holds data hostage — encrypts files and demands payment (usually cryptocurrency) for the decryption key.

questions.q: A rootkit is designed to?
options: Hide malware presence from the operating system
questions.answer: Hide malware presence from the operating system
questions.explanation: Rootkits embed deeply into OS — hiding processes, files, and network connections from security tools.

questions.q: Spyware primarily aims to?
options: Collect and exfiltrate user data without consent
questions.answer: Collect and exfiltrate user data without consent
questions.explanation: Spyware silently collects user data — browsing habits, credentials, financial info — and sends to attacker.

questions.q: A botnet is?
options: Collection of compromised computers controlled by an attacker
questions.answer: Collection of compromised computers controlled by an attacker
questions.explanation: Botnets are armies of compromised machines (bots) controlled via C&C (command and control) servers — used for DDoS, spam, mining.

questions.q: A keylogger records?
options: All keystrokes typed by the user
questions.answer: All keystrokes typed by the user
questions.explanation: Keyloggers capture every keystroke — including passwords, credit card numbers, and private messages.

questions.q: Phishing differs from spear phishing because?
options: Spear phishing is targeted at specific individuals or organizations
questions.answer: Spear phishing is targeted at specific individuals or organizations
questions.explanation: Phishing = mass generic emails. Spear phishing = tailored, targeted attack using personal information about the victim.

questions.q: A vishing attack uses what medium?
questions.explanation: Vishing = voice phishing. Attackers call pretending to be IT support, banks, or government agencies.

questions.q: Smishing attacks use which medium?
questions.explanation: Smishing = SMS phishing. Malicious texts urge victims to click links or call numbers.

questions.q: An attacker holds a door open for employees to enter a secure facility without badging in. This is called?
questions.explanation: Tailgating exploits social courtesy — following authorized personnel through secure doors without using credentials.

questions.q: Pretexting in social engineering means?
options: Creating a fabricated scenario to extract information
questions.answer: Creating a fabricated scenario to extract information
questions.explanation: Pretexting creates a false identity or scenario to manipulate targets — 'I'm from IT, I need your password to fix a problem.'

questions.q: Which social engineering attack retrieves sensitive information discarded in garbage?
questions.explanation: Dumpster diving searches through physical trash for sensitive documents, passwords, or device information.

questions.q: The most effective defense against social engineering is?
questions.explanation: Social engineering targets humans — training employees to recognize and resist manipulation is the primary defense.

questions.q: Pharming redirects users to malicious websites by?
options: Corrupting DNS records or hosts file
questions.answer: Corrupting DNS records or hosts file
questions.explanation: Pharming manipulates DNS — victims are redirected to fraudulent sites even when typing the correct URL.

questions.q: HTTP and HTTPS operate at which OSI layer?
questions.explanation: HTTP (port 80) and HTTPS (port 443) are Application layer (Layer 7) protocols used for web traffic.

questions.q: Which OSI layer does Telnet operate at?
questions.explanation: Telnet is an Application layer protocol (port 23) used for remote terminal access — insecure, replaced by SSH.

questions.q: SSH (Secure Shell) operates at which OSI layer?
questions.explanation: SSH is an Application layer (Layer 7) protocol on port 22, providing encrypted remote access.

questions.q: DHCP operates at which OSI layer?
questions.explanation: DHCP (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol) is a Layer 7 Application protocol using UDP ports 67/68 to assign IP addresses.

questions.q: SNMP (Simple Network Management Protocol) operates at which layer?
questions.explanation: SNMP is an Application layer protocol (UDP port 161/162) used to monitor and manage network devices.

questions.q: Which layer does UDP operate at?
questions.explanation: UDP (User Datagram Protocol) is a Transport layer (Layer 4) protocol — connectionless, faster than TCP but no delivery guarantee.

questions.q: IP (Internet Protocol) operates at which OSI layer?
questions.explanation: IP operates at Layer 3 (Network), providing logical addressing and routing between networks.

questions.q: Ethernet operates at which OSI layers?
questions.explanation: Ethernet spans both Physical (Layer 1) for signal transmission and Data Link (Layer 2) for MAC addressing and framing.

questions.q: A bridge operates at which OSI layer?
questions.explanation: Bridges operate at Layer 2 (Data Link), forwarding frames based on MAC addresses. They segment collision domains.

questions.q: Which OSI layer is responsible for establishing, managing, and terminating sessions between applications?
questions.explanation: Layer 5 (Session) manages sessions — it sets up, coordinates, and terminates conversations between applications.

questions.q: What is the correct order of PDU encapsulation as data travels DOWN the OSI stack?
questions.explanation: Encapsulation order (sending): Application data → Transport adds header (Segment) → Network adds IP (Packet) → Data Link adds MAC (Frame) → Physical converts to Bits.

questions.q: What is the PDU at the Transport layer?
questions.explanation: Transport layer PDU = Segment. TCP segments contain sequence numbers, port numbers, and payload data.

questions.q: At which OSI layer is a trailer (like CRC/FCS) added to data?
questions.explanation: The Data Link layer adds both a header (source/dest MAC) and a trailer (CRC/FCS for error detection) to create a frame.

questions.q: When data is received and travels UP the OSI stack, what is this process called?
questions.explanation: Decapsulation is the process of removing headers as data travels up the OSI stack from Physical to Application layer.

questions.q: A SYN flood attack targets which OSI layer?
questions.explanation: SYN flood attacks target Layer 4 (Transport) by sending thousands of TCP SYN requests without completing the handshake, exhausting connection tables.

questions.q: A ping flood (ICMP flood) attack targets which OSI layer?
questions.explanation: Ping floods use ICMP packets which operate at Layer 3 (Network). The attacker overwhelms the target with ICMP Echo Requests.

questions.q: An HTTP DDoS attack targeting a web server primarily targets which OSI layer?
questions.explanation: HTTP DDoS (like Slowloris or GET floods) are Layer 7 Application attacks. They are harder to filter than lower-layer attacks.

questions.q: A man-in-the-middle (MitM) attack that intercepts unencrypted data primarily exploits which OSI layer weakness?
questions.explanation: MitM attacks often exploit the Presentation layer (Layer 6) — the lack of encryption means intercepted data is readable. SSL stripping is a Layer 6 attack.

questions.q: ARP spoofing attacks target which OSI layer?
questions.explanation: ARP operates at Layer 2. ARP spoofing poisons ARP caches with fake MAC address mappings, enabling MitM attacks on local networks.

questions.q: A Ping of Death attack sends oversized ICMP packets. Which OSI layer does this exploit?
questions.explanation: Ping of Death exploits the ICMP protocol at Layer 3 (Network). Packets exceeding 65,535 bytes cause buffer overflows on vulnerable systems.

questions.q: WPA2 encryption for Wi-Fi primarily operates at which OSI layer?
questions.explanation: WPA2 encrypts at Layer 2 (Data Link). The wireless frame payload is encrypted before being transmitted over the physical medium.

questions.q: IPSec operates at which OSI layer?
questions.explanation: IPSec is a Layer 3 (Network) security protocol suite that provides encryption and authentication for IP packets. Used in VPNs.

questions.q: TLS (Transport Layer Security) actually operates at which OSI layer?
questions.explanation: Despite its name, TLS operates at Layer 6 (Presentation). It handles encryption/decryption of data before passing to the Application layer.

questions.q: A WAF (Web Application Firewall) inspects traffic at which layer?
questions.explanation: WAFs operate at Layer 7 (Application), inspecting HTTP/HTTPS content for SQL injection, XSS, and other application-layer attacks.

questions.q: Which statement about ARP is TRUE regarding the OSI model?
questions.explanation: ARP bridges Layers 2 and 3 — it takes a Layer 3 IP address as input and returns a Layer 2 MAC address. It is often classified as Layer 2 or 2.5.

questions.q: You need to troubleshoot a connectivity issue. A host can ping 127.0.0.1 but cannot ping its default gateway. Which OSI layer should you investigate?
questions.explanation: Pinging 127.0.0.1 confirms TCP/IP stack is working (Layers 3-7 are functional). Cannot reach the gateway = likely a Layer 2 (MAC/ARP) or Layer 1 (cable/NIC) issue. Layer 2 is the best answer here.

questions.q: A switch learns MAC addresses and builds its CAM table. This is a function of which OSI layer?
questions.explanation: Switches are Layer 2 devices. They learn MAC addresses by inspecting incoming frames and populate their Content Addressable Memory (CAM) table.

questions.q: Which OSI layer is most directly responsible for port numbers (like port 80, 443, 22)?
questions.explanation: Port numbers are a Layer 4 (Transport) concept. TCP and UDP headers include source and destination port numbers to identify services.

questions.q: Data format translation (e.g., ASCII to EBCDIC, JPEG compression) occurs at which OSI layer?
questions.explanation: The Presentation layer (Layer 6) handles data format translation, encoding (ASCII/Unicode), and compression so different systems can communicate.

questions.q: A multilayer switch routes traffic between VLANs. When doing inter-VLAN routing, it acts at which OSI layer?
questions.explanation: Multilayer switches perform Layer 2 switching within VLANs AND Layer 3 routing between VLANs — hence "multilayer."

questions.q: The TCP/IP model has how many layers?
questions.explanation: The TCP/IP model has 4 layers: Application, Transport, Internet, and Network Access (also called Link).

questions.q: Which OSI layers map to the TCP/IP Application layer?
questions.explanation: TCP/IP Application layer = OSI Session (5) + Presentation (6) + Application (7). HTTP, DNS, FTP, SMTP all live here.

questions.q: Which OSI layers map to the TCP/IP Network Access layer?
questions.explanation: TCP/IP Network Access (Link) layer = OSI Physical (1) + Data Link (2). It handles hardware addressing and physical transmission.

questions.q: The TCP/IP Internet layer corresponds to which OSI layer?
questions.explanation: TCP/IP Internet layer = OSI Network layer (Layer 3). IP, ICMP, and routing protocols operate here.

questions.q: The TCP/IP Transport layer corresponds to which OSI layer?
questions.explanation: TCP/IP Transport layer = OSI Transport layer (Layer 4). TCP and UDP both operate at this layer.

questions.q: In the TCP/IP model, which layer is responsible for logical addressing and routing?
questions.explanation: The Internet layer handles logical (IP) addressing and routing — equivalent to OSI Layer 3 (Network).

questions.q: HTTP operates at which TCP/IP layer?
questions.explanation: HTTP is an Application layer protocol in TCP/IP. The TCP/IP Application layer encompasses OSI layers 5, 6, and 7.

questions.q: TCP operates at which TCP/IP layer?
questions.explanation: TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) operates at the TCP/IP Transport layer, providing reliable, connection-oriented communication.

questions.q: Which TCP/IP layer handles MAC addresses and physical transmission?
questions.explanation: The Network Access (Link) layer handles MAC addressing and physical transmission, combining OSI Layers 1 and 2.

questions.q: ICMP (used by ping) operates at which TCP/IP layer?
questions.explanation: ICMP operates at the TCP/IP Internet layer (= OSI Layer 3). Ping uses ICMP Echo Request/Reply messages.

questions.q: What is the main advantage of the TCP/IP model over the OSI model?
options: It is a theoretical model only
It is a practical, implemented model used on the internet
questions.answer: It is a practical, implemented model used on the internet
questions.explanation: The TCP/IP model is the actual protocol suite used on the internet. The OSI model is a theoretical reference model for understanding network communication.

questions.q: Which model is considered a theoretical reference model rather than an implementation?
questions.explanation: The OSI model is a conceptual reference framework. The TCP/IP model (also called DoD model) is the practical implementation used in real networks.

questions.q: The TCP/IP model is also known as the:
questions.explanation: The TCP/IP model is called the DoD (Department of Defense) model because it was developed by DARPA for the US military ARPANET project.

questions.q: DNS resolves hostnames to IP addresses. At which TCP/IP layer does this occur?
questions.explanation: DNS is an Application layer service in TCP/IP (port 53, UDP). It maps human-readable names to IP addresses.

questions.q: A three-way TCP handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) occurs at which TCP/IP layer?
questions.explanation: The TCP three-way handshake is a Transport layer function in TCP/IP, establishing a reliable connection before data exchange.

questions.q: IPSec encrypts IP packets. At which TCP/IP layer does IPSec operate?
questions.explanation: IPSec operates at the Internet layer (= OSI Layer 3), encrypting and authenticating IP packets. It is commonly used in VPNs.

questions.q: Which TCP/IP layer combines the functions of OSI Session, Presentation, and Application layers?
questions.explanation: TCP/IP Application layer is a "rolled up" version of OSI Layers 5 (Session), 6 (Presentation), and 7 (Application).

questions.q: Ethernet framing and MAC addressing are functions of which TCP/IP layer?
questions.explanation: Ethernet (MAC addressing, framing, CRC) is a Network Access layer function — the TCP/IP equivalent of OSI Data Link + Physical.

questions.q: A router makes forwarding decisions at which TCP/IP layer?
questions.explanation: Routers operate at the Internet layer, using IP addresses to make forwarding decisions — equivalent to OSI Layer 3.

questions.q: FTP uses TCP for reliability. When FTP data is being transferred, which TWO TCP/IP layers are most directly involved?
questions.explanation: FTP is an Application layer protocol. It uses TCP at the Transport layer for reliable delivery. Both layers are directly involved in the transfer.

questions.q: Which statement correctly maps TCP/IP to OSI? Select the MOST accurate.
options: TCP/IP Network Access = OSI Layers 2, 3, and 4
questions.explanation: The correct mappings: Network Access=L1+L2, Internet=L3, Transport=L4, Application=L5+L6+L7.

questions.q: UDP is connectionless and provides no delivery guarantee. It operates at which TCP/IP layer?
questions.explanation: UDP operates at the TCP/IP Transport layer, alongside TCP. Unlike TCP, UDP has no handshake, sequencing, or acknowledgment.

questions.q: ARP resolves IP addresses to MAC addresses. At which TCP/IP layer does ARP operate?
questions.explanation: ARP operates at the Network Access layer in TCP/IP (bridging Internet and Network Access), resolving Internet layer IPs to Network Access layer MACs.

questions.q: Which layer of the TCP/IP model would be most affected by a NIC (Network Interface Card) failure?
questions.explanation: A NIC failure affects the Network Access layer — the physical hardware interface responsible for sending/receiving frames.

questions.q: TLS/SSL encrypts web traffic. At which TCP/IP layer does this encryption occur?
questions.explanation: In the TCP/IP model, TLS/SSL is considered part of the Application layer (TCP/IP collapses OSI Presentation+Session+Application into one layer).
